content:
3 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

​Let’s be real, AI is part of the job now. Finding a dev who isn’t leaning on it daily is like finding one who doesn’t use Stack Overflow. If you’re looking at a PR these days, an LLM probably touched it.
But this year feels different. We’ve moved past basic autocomplete and “fix my regex” prompts. The new stuff feels like having a teammate, not just a plugin. They plan, test, and debug across your whole repo while you think about the architecture.
If “agentic” AI still sounds like marketing hype to you, check out our Agentic AI primer to see why it actually matters.
I spent a few weeks living in these tools. No fluff, just what works, what’s a pain, and where you’re going to waste your money.
How I actually tested these
I didn’t care about the demos. I looked at the stuff that matters: does it break my setup? Is the code actually good? Is the pricing a nightmare? Basically, is it helping me ship or just getting in my way?
The 2026 short-list
Claude Code is the absolute best I’ve used for deep logic. It costs, but the reasoning is light-years ahead. GitHub Copilot is still the default for that flow state, even with the new usage-based bills. If you want an IDE that actually “gets” AI, Cursor is the only real choice it starts around £20/mo and feels native.
If your legal team is paranoid, Tabnine is the way to go for privacy. Need to ship a prototype from a browser? Replit is still the king of speed. And if you want something light and proactive without the big company baggage, Windsurf is a great, cheaper alternative.
The honest truth on each
Claude Code is a monster in a massive repo. It’s more than just chat; it plans multi-file changes and runs your tests. Just be careful if you aren’t specific, it likes to over-engineer things.
Copilot handles the boring stuff, boilerplate, PR summaries, and Jira tickets perfectly. But Cursor is VS Code with a brain. It refactors whole features and hooks into AWS and Figma. It’s a dream for full-stack work.
Tabnine stays local. It learns your team’s patterns without sharing your data. Replit is fast, but watch those credits it gets expensive for big projects.
Building your own AI? Use these

• LangChain / LangGraph: The guts of most AI agents.

• Ollama: Run models locally. Save your API budget.

• CrewAI: When you need a whole squad of agents working together.

• Gemini Code Assist: Great if you’re already living in Google Cloud.
How to pick one without the headache
Ask yourself: do you want a helper or a partner? Completion is one thing; autonomy is another. Watch the budget fees add up fast. Just pick the one that makes you ship faster, not the one with the best marketing.
At the end of the day, the best tool is the one that actually helps you get code out the door.
My ground rules for AI coding

• Don’t trust the bot. Review every line before you merge.

• Set billing alerts immediately. Trust me.

• Don’t leak company secrets to public models.

• Use it to get better, not lazier. You’re still the pilot.
Wrapping up
There’s no “right” answer here. Pick the tool that fits your project. Whether you want Copilot’s ease of use or Claude’s brainpower, just pick one and go.
What are you actually using? Let me know what’s working for you.
Quick Answers
What’s the best way to do this for free?
Ollama. Run it on your own hardware, pay nothing.
Is Copilot still worth it?
Yeah, if you can deal with the new billing. It’s still the smoothest workflow.
Claude Code vs. Cursor?
Claude for big-brain logic. Cursor for an editor that lives and breathes AI.
Is my job safe?
Yes. It just means you spend more time on actual architecture and less time writing boilerplate.
​
